one_line_takeaway: The first ground-truth measurement of the underground market for malicious LLM services: it is real, growing, and monetized (WormGPT ~$28K/2mo), but current Mallas mostly produce shallow, easily-spotted malicious content rather than novel operational attacks.
key_finding: Malla is a real, growing, and profitable cybercrime segment. Eight backend LLMs power the ecosystem; OpenAI is the most-targeted vendor and gpt-3.5-turbo the most-abused API (174 of the Malla projects). Uncensored code-generating Mallas can produce compilable, VirusTotal-evading malicious code: EscapeGPT and DarkGPT led with ~66.67% and ~65.08% code compilability and high evasiveness (0.67 and 0.63), and EscapeGPT scored 1.00/1.00/1.00 on phishing-site format/validity/evasiveness. WormGPT alone netted ~$28,325 over two months. However, capabilities are shallow: generated CVE exploits did not actually compromise a real vulnerable VICIdial target, and a human study found Malla-generated phishing was MORE suspicious (78.05% of Malla emails flagged as phishing vs 69.32% for non-Malla), i.e., lower quality than human-made phishing. No Malla-generated content could be attributed to real-world attacks (max cosine similarity 0.64 emails / 0.84 sites, below the 0.9 threshold).
core_contribution: The first systematic empirical study of real-world 'Malla' (malicious LLM-integrated applications/services sold in the cybercrime underground). The authors' own contribution is a measurement of 212 real-world Mallas collected Feb-Sep 2023 (14 commercial Malla services and 198 publicly hosted Malla projects on Poe/FlowGPT), characterizing the underground ecosystem (vendors, hosting, pricing, revenue), the quality of malicious content Mallas generate, and the two construction techniques miscreants use (wrapping uncensored LLMs, and jailbreaking public LLM APIs). They also release artifacts: 45 malicious prompts, eight identified backend LLMs, and 182 jailbreak prompts. Note: the LLM-authorship-attribution technique and the 'Ignore the previous instructions' prompt-leaking attack are adopted from prior work ([88,123] and Perez et al. [115]), not invented here; the contribution is applying them to fingerprint real Mallas.
threat_model: A miscreant with only ordinary-user capabilities (no special access to or privileges over commercial pretrained LLMs) violates LLM usage policies to build and sell a malicious LLM application. They either (a) wrap an uncensored/open LLM (e.g., Luna AI Llama2 Uncensored, Pygmalion-13B, deprecated Davinci-002/003) as a service, or (b) jailbreak a public commercial LLM API (e.g., OpenAI gpt-3.5-turbo) with crafted prompts to bypass content moderation. The Malla is deployed as a web service or on a third-party LLMA hosting platform, promoted on underground marketplaces/forums, purchased (often via crypto), and used to generate malicious code, phishing emails, and scam/phishing websites.
limitations: Sampling bias: findings rest on Mallas the authors could observe and on LLMs/jailbreak prompts they could fingerprint. Several purchase attempts were themselves scams (WormGPT, FraudGPT, BLACKHATGPT never delivered access), so 'samples' skew to what could be obtained. Quality metrics measure content quality, not actual real-world effectiveness/maliciousness (acknowledged). The user study stripped sender addresses and URLs, which the authors note may over- or under-estimate real deception. Scope is limited to Python/C(++) code and three functionalities (malicious code, phishing email, phishing site); other advertised abuses (misinformation, card-info collection) lacked evidence and were excluded. Backend-LLM inference for three services is a classifier estimate (precision/recall 0.87), not ground truth. Data is a 2023 snapshot; several backends (Davinci-002/003) were since deprecated.

datasets_used: 13,353 listings scraped from nine underground marketplaces/forums (Abacus, Kerberos, Kingdom, WeTheNorth, MGM Grand, Hack Forums, XSS.is, BreachForums, BlackHatWorld)
25 Malla service listings -> 14 Malla services -> 9 obtained Malla samples
198 Malla projects (125 from Poe, 73 from FlowGPT)
45 malicious prompts (Pm) extracted from listing screenshots; 182 jailbreak prompts; 744 public jailbreak prompts (Pr) for comparison
Enron Email Dataset (benign emails), Confense (non-Malla phishing emails), PhishTank (non-Malla phishing sites, 36,343 webpages for attribution)
CVE-2022-34877/34878 (SQLi) and CVE-2022-34819 (buffer overflow) tested on live VICIdial (VICIbox v9.0.3) and OWASP WebGoat 7.1
15,114 LLM responses used to train the authorship-attribution classifier
quantitative_results: 212 real-world Mallas total (14 services + 198 projects); eight backend LLMs; 182 jailbreak prompts released
Backend LLMs identified: OpenAI GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Davinci-002, Davinci-003; Anthropic Claude-instant, Claude-2-100k; Pygmalion-13B; Luna AI Llama2 Uncensored
gpt-3.5-turbo used by 174 Malla projects (most abused); Claude-instant 14, GPT-4 6, Claude-2-100k 2, Pygmalion-13B 2
WormGPT revenue: $28,325 over ~2 months ($26,783 BTC + $1,542 ETH), ~$15,965/month; 27 BTC + 57 ETH incoming transactions
Pricing: $5-$199/month; malicious-code Mallas cheaper than a traditional malware vendor (Charybdis Worm $399)
Malicious-code quality (compilability C / evasiveness E): EscapeGPT 0.67/0.67, DarkGPT 0.65/0.63, Evil-GPT 0.57/0.52, WolfGPT 0.52/0.52; EscapeGPT phishing-site F/V/E = 1.00/1.00/1.00
Phishing email: WolfGPT readability 1.00, evasiveness 0.67; WolfGPT emails avg 67.47 words / 3.07 spam words vs others 196.32 words / 8.15 spam words
Exploits: 22/50 CVE exploits compilable; none succeeded against live VICIdial; 7 (from XXXGPT, BadGPT) compromised generic OWASP WebGoat targets
User study: Malla phishing emails flagged as phishing 78.05% vs 69.32% non-Malla vs 39.20% benign; sites 72.93% vs 57.92% vs 44.81%

Reverse engineering: authorship classifier precision/recall 0.87 (5-fold CV); prompt-leaking attack 93.01% success, Jaro-Winkler 0.88, semantic similarity 0.83; recovered 52 jailbreak prompts from 54 projects
Real-world attribution: max cosine similarity 0.64 (emails) and 0.84 (webpages), both below 0.9 threshold -> no attributable real-world use
Longevity: 24/25 listings still active; only 4/9 services operational by March 2024; ~69.6% Poe / 86.3% FlowGPT projects remained after disclosure
baselines_compared: Malla services vs Malla projects vs raw uncensored LLMs (Appendix A) on the five quality metrics
Malla-generated phishing vs non-Malla (human) phishing (Confense emails, PhishTank sites) vs benign (Enron emails, legitimate sites) in the MTurk study
Malla jailbreak prompts vs 744 public jailbreak prompts (topic-term and generated-content similarity)
Malla malicious-code pricing vs traditional malware vendor pricing ($399 Charybdis Worm)
VirusTotal (code/site detection) and OOPSpam (email spam detection) as the evasion baselines
